summary: Synthetic weak-to-strong generalization study mapping performance gap recovery (PGR) vs imitation across supervisor capacity and noise levels. Found imitation-dominant regime with mean PGR=0.038 and imitation score=0.716 across all conditions, suggesting naive weak-label training primarily learns supervisor errors rather than eliciting capabilities.
claims: Naive weak-label finetuning in synthetic settings strongly favors imitation over capability recovery (mean imitation=0.716 vs mean PGR=0.038)
All tested regime conditions (3x3 grid of capacity/noise levels) showed high imitation (>0.6) and low PGR (<0.1)
Higher-capacity supervisors paradoxically yielded worse PGR (0.0-0.10) than low-capacity ones (0.05-0.07)
Systematic label noise maintains imitation behavior with minimal impact on error agreement rates
Large performance gaps (17-37 percentage points) between weak/strong models provide room for recovery but PGR remains minimal

evidence.summary: Detailed experimental results showing PGR and imitation scores for 9 capacity/noise combinations
Statistical summary of regime map with means, standard deviations, and regime counts
Visual regime maps showing PGR and imitation score heatmaps across parameter space

limitations: Small synthetic dataset (500 samples, 10 features) limits generalizability to real W2S scenarios
Fixed strong student architecture same as baseline may bias toward imitation over capability elicitation
Coarse 3x3 parameter grid may miss finer regime boundaries or transition zones
Simple systematic noise model (label flipping) may not capture complex real supervisor error patterns
Single random seed evaluation provides limited statistical robustness
Short training periods (max 300 iterations) may not allow sufficient time for capability recovery
next: Test confidence-weighted training using supervisor uncertainty to reduce imitation pressure
Evaluate architectural diversity between supervisors and students to promote capability transfer
Expand to real datasets and tasks to validate synthetic regime findings
Study training dynamics to identify when imitation behavior emerges during optimization
Investigate multi-supervisor ensembles to dilute systematic error patterns
